name: Platform-Neutral Access Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish governance baseline and baseline pilots for platform-neutral fallback → Systemic: platform-dependency on Apple/Google trust signals decreases by 25% (share of authentication flows not relying on native platform signals) and procurement costs rise by 3% due to diversification → Strategic: Denmark gains sovereignty and resilience, but faces longer certification cycles and higher governance overhead. Trade-offs: faster rollout is sacrificed for resilience and broader supplier options. Measurement: progress tracked by changes in dependency metrics and procurement overhead over 24 months.
options: Adopt a formal platform-neutral access governance framework that binds agencies to include a fallback authentication requirement in procurement and standards alignment
Pilot a phased implementation of platform-neutral fallback components across select ministries, with independent testing against NSIS and OpenID Connect-based specs
Launch a government-backed platform-neutral identity marketplace that funds multiple fallback providers via a tokenized incentive scheme and SSI-style attestations
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider protracted certification cycles.

name: Procurement-Neutrality Integration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate a structured procurement reform to embed platform-neutral fallback requirements across new contracts → Systemic: diversification reduces single-vendor risk by 30% and compliance costs adjust by +2% due to additional testing → Strategic: resilience improves but bureaucratic friction increases and pace slows. Trade-offs: increased governance overhead vs faster problem resolution. Measurement: track vendor diversity and contract cycle times.
options: Embed platform-neutral fallback requirements into all new procurements as a standard clause and establish a cross-agency review board
Establish phased procurement pilots with two alternate fallback pathways, validated through interoperability test suites and independent verification
Create an as-a-service identity marketplace funded by a government-backed pay-for-performance model with vendor-agnostic certification for secure fallback providers
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider protracted certification cycles.

name: Standards-Pressure and EU Alignment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate joint EU/NSIS standardization efforts for platform-neutral fallback and interoperable attestations → Systemic: cross-border interoperability improves by 40% but certification overhead increases by 5% for compliance regimes → Strategic: Denmark gains credible influence but faces heavier regulatory compliance burden. Trade-offs: cross-border alignment vs national autonomy. Measurement: monitor interoperability adoption rate and certification timeline.
options: Engage with NSIS and EU wallet standards to secure Danish alignment and avoid vendor lock-in
Formalize EU working-group participation to define interoperability tests and platform-neutral fallback requirements for AltID procurement
Lead Nordic-EU standardization using verifiable credentials and a blockchain-backed attestation ledger with a shared certification model
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider protracted certification cycles.

name: Public-Private Coalition and Resilience Framing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Launch a cross-sector coalition and resilience framing program with a baseline policy proposal for procurement neutrality → Systemic: coalition participation increases resilience sentiment by 35% in stakeholder surveys and policy influence expands, but governance complexity grows with more actors, leading to +4% admin cost → Strategic: stronger legitimacy and procurement influence but slower consensus-building. Trade-offs: breadth vs depth. Measurement: track coalition size and policy adoption.
options: Establish advisory coalitions across civil society, academia, and regulators to frame resilience narratives and procurement criteria
Publish resilience-focused procurement guidelines and require risk assessments quantifying supplier concentration
Create a cross-agency, cross-party alliance with shared IP assets and a venture-like fund to co-develop platform-neutral fallback components
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider protracted certification cycles.

name: Demonstrator-Centric Validation Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Run non-production demonstrations to validate fallback feasibility under simulated conditions → Systemic: two live demonstrators achieve 95–98% pass rates in tests and risk signals reduced by 20%, but require ongoing external funding of +6% → Strategic: credible evidence improves policy and procurement leverage but ongoing funding and external review remain necessary. Trade-offs: realism vs control. Measurement: track demonstrator success rate and funding adequacy.
options: Conduct non-production demonstrations in controlled environments to validate fallback feasibility
Develop two working demonstrators (mobile and browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback) with independent evaluation and public results
Institute an open, pay-for-performance sandbox as-a-service for platform-neutral fallback identity, funded by a shared fund and open-market vendor competition
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider protracted certification cycles.

name: Sovereign-Identity Platform-Decoupling Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A platform-decoupling task force is activated to map fallback authentication flows and draft procurement language toward platform-neutral signals. → Systemic: Platform-dependency exposure declines by 25% as measured by the share of trust-critical signals sourced from internal or EU-certified components; Trade-off: platform sovereignty and resilience vs speed-to-market and procurement agility. → Strategic: Denmark gains sovereignty and resilience, enabling stronger EU alignment, but near-term service integration may slow as standards mature and vendor negotiations lengthen.
options: Establish a platform-decoupling task force to map fallback authentication flows and draft procurement language toward platform-neutral signals.
Pilot a vendor-neutral attestation layer using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect in a controlled pilot with two government agencies.
Deploy a distributed identity vault based on DID/SSI with verifiable credentials to create platform-agnostic trust anchors and blockchain-backed attestations.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider ongoing regulatory approval timelines and cross-government coordination fatigue.

name: Open Wallet Interoperability and Contingency Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Launch a standards-based baseline for open-wallet interoperability and NSIS alignment, triggering policy updates and initial procurement edits. → Systemic: Proportion of identity attestations that can be fulfilled by non-proprietary wallets increases by 30 percentage points; Trade-off: interoperability gains vs ecosystem disruption and vendor pushback. → Strategic: Denmark gains broader resilience and EU compatibility but accepts longer governance negotiations and slower near-term rollout.
options: Launch a standards-based baseline for open-wallet interoperability and NSIS alignment, triggering policy updates and initial procurement edits.
Convene a cross-sector procurement consortium to specify platform-neutral fallback criteria in RFPs, with pilots in 3 agencies.
Launch an EU-wide Open Wallet Consortium to establish cross-border wallet interoperability using DID/SSI and a shared verifiable-credentials framework.
review: Controls interoperability vs vendor ecosystem disruption. Weakness: The options fail to consider practical vendor migration timelines and risk of standards fragmentation.

name: Procurement-Neutral Standards-Leverage Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Publish a standards-forward policy note to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advocating for platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurements. → Systemic: Procurement openness increases by 20% and lock-in risk reduces; Trade-off: standards-driven neutrality vs speed-to-contract and incumbent vendor influence. → Strategic: Builds long-term leverage for neutral architecture but may slow short-term supplier onboarding.
options: Publish a standards-forward policy note to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advocating for platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurements.
Incorporate a pre-commercial procurement framework for fallback mechanisms with clear milestones.
Create a standing, buyer-led Standards and Innovation Council that licenses a platform-neutral trust framework as open-source under a shared-risk consortium model.
review: Controls standards-driven neutrality vs speed-to-contract. Weakness: The options fail to consider prolonged governance cycles and potential political resistance.

name: AltID-Alignment and EU Wallet Standards Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Map EU wallet requirements and propose Danish alignment within AltID policy documents. → Systemic: 25% alignment to EU wallet guidelines; Trade-off: centralization of standards vs national sovereignty. → Strategic: Denmark increases influence in EU standard-setting but may limit local customization and procurement benefits.
options: Map EU wallet requirements and propose Danish alignment within AltID policy documents.
Propose a joint EU-supported sandbox for AltID integration with defined legal and technical pathways.
Lead an EU-wide pilot deploying a cross-border SSI wallet anchored to a European Open Identity Network using verifiable credentials.
review: Controls EU alignment vs national sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to ensure enforceable cross-border compliance and funding stability.

name: Resilience-First Access Architecture and Continuity Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Establish a two-anchor redundancy plan and begin quarterly failover drills. → Systemic: Outage MTTR improves by 40% across critical services; Trade-off: resilience vs cost and ongoing maintenance overhead. → Strategic: Strengthens continuity for essential services but increases governance and operational overhead.
options: Develop a crisis-communication and continuity-testing plan for access during platform outages.
Implement a redundancy architecture with two independent trust anchors and quarterly failover drills.
Introduce a mesh network of identity verifiers leveraging local offline credential caches and edge validation using secure enclaves to provide offline fallback.
review: Controls resilience vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term scalability and the need for standardized offline credential management.

name: AltID-Open-Standards Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Agencies adopt a platform-neutral reference architecture in procurement and evaluation criteria → Systemic: Supplier diversity increases by 25%, single-vendor dependency falls by 40%, and procurement cycle time rises by 12% due to added conformity checks; trade-off: resilience and autonomy increase at the cost of governance burden → Strategic: Over 2–3 years, Denmark gains greater digital sovereignty and continuity against platform gatekeepers, with ongoing governance costs. Trade-offs: Governance overhead may slow urgent procurements; increased administration is required to sustain standards. Strategic impact is contingent on sustained funding for standards maintenance.
options: Mandate open-standards governance across procurement to enforce a platform-neutral reference architecture and certification criteria.
Pilot a phased procurement framework that requires platform-neutral interoperability for all identity services.
Launch a community-owned fallback identity framework that tests open credentials in real public-sector workflows.
review: Controls Platform-Dependency Risk vs. Procurement Agility. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy-preserving cross-border data sharing in a platform-neutral framework.

name: Platform-Neutral Fallback-Orchestration Strategy
consequences: Immediate: A platform-neutral fallback authentication path is defined for pilot use and a governance plan is established → Systemic: Fallback readiness improves by 40% and service availability extends by 6 hours during outages; cost of integration and token infrastructure rises by 15% → Strategic: In the medium term, resilience against OS gatekeepers improves and continuity risks are reduced, but sustainable funding and coordination are required. Trade-off: faster failover pathways may introduce added surface area for attacks and duplicate controls.
options: Mandate a cross-agency fallback authentication path using open standards in pilot deployments.
Pilot a staged rollout with privacy-preserving data minimization and vendor-agnostic integration.
Launch a token-based fallback-as-a-service toolkit with an open-source core and a paid, platform-agnostic support model.
review: Controls Platform-Dependency Risk vs. Implementation Cadence. Weakness: The options fail to consider secure hardware token standardization across devices.

name: Interoperability Standards Push for AltID Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Interoperability standards are embedded in AltID procurement and pilot specifications → Systemic: Cross-border wallet interoperability improves by 25%, certification overhead increases by 30%, and project budgets expand accordingly; trade-off: agility and time-to-implementation suffer in exchange for compliance and portability → Strategic: Denmark aligns with EU wallet requirements, reduces market-entry risks for vendors, and strengthens long-run interoperability. Trade-off: regulatory burden may slow local innovation and raise costs for smaller vendors.
options: Mandate interoperability certification in AltID procurement and pilot specs.
Pilot EU-aligned cross-border wallet tests with staggered timelines.
Launch a decentralized validator marketplace with open connectors to certify cross-border attestations.
review: Controls Interoperability vs. Certification Overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider data portability beyond EU boundaries.

name: Civil-Society Coalition and Open-Provisioning Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Civil-society coalitions co-design procurement language and policy proposals → Systemic: Public trust rises by 5 points in sentiment surveys, and stakeholder leverage increases by ~18% in negotiations; trade-off: coalition complexity may slow decision-making and expand validation steps → Strategic: The policy environment becomes more receptive to platform-neutral approaches, enabling more credible standards pressure and procurement language changes. Trade-off: broader engagement can dilute execution speed and create competing interests.
options: Mandate civil-society co-design sessions in procurement language and policy proposals.
Pilot open provisioning guidelines with public dashboards and stakeholder feedback loops.
Launch a cooperative funding model for platform-neutral identity services with community governance.
review: Controls Coalition Engagement vs. Policy Paralysis. Weakness: The options fail to consider divergent privacy norms across civil society groups.

name: European Digital Identity Wallet Certification Alignment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: EU wallet standards inputs are reflected in national procurement and design reviews → Systemic: Cross-border wallet compatibility improves by 15%, compliance costs rise by 10–20% for national vendors, and regulatory alignment strengthens; trade-off: domestic vendors face higher costs and longer lead times → Strategic: Denmark remains aligned with EU innovation timelines, improving export potential and interoperability, though budgetary pressure for compliance rises. Trade-off: national capability to absorb EU-compliant costs may vary by agency size.
options: Incorporate EU wallet standards into national procurement and certification processes.
Pilot cross-border interoperability tests with EU Wallet partners and simulate joint procurement.
Create a paid, shared services platform offering EU-wallet compliant, platform-neutral identity as-a-service for public bodies.
review: Controls EU Alignment vs. Domestic Vendor Readiness. Weakness: The options fail to consider small vendors’ capacity to meet EU-compliant standards.
